original_abstract: This study examines the demographic, institutional, and economic characteristics of the individuals responsible for over 750 of science’s most significant breakthroughs, encompassing both Nobel Prize winners and major non-Nobel discoverers. By analyzing a comprehensive dataset of 982 scientists across history, the research identifies a major shift in the profile of the scientific elite toward greater interdisciplinarity, institutional elitism, and more advanced age. Findings reveal that 54% of Nobel-winning discoveries were made by researchers with degrees in at least two different fields, suggesting that interdisciplinary training provides the methodological versatility essential for innovation. The results further indicate that science is increasingly concentrated in elite environments, with 30% of major discoveries occurring at the world’s top 25 universities. Additionally, the age at which scientists make significant contributions has risen: while historically common at younger ages, major discoveries by scientists over 60 have become exceptionally rare, with the current "golden age" for productivity falling between 35 and 45. The study also highlights persistent disparities, noting the continued underrepresentation of women and a decline in the dominance of researchers based in Europe in favor of North America. These trends suggest that while the "burden of knowledge" requires longer periods of training and specialized environments, scientific progress could be further accelerated if funding agencies and academic institutions countered narrow specialization by incentivizing interdisciplinary research and broader methodological integration.
humanized_abstract: What unites the 750+ individuals behind some of the most important achievements in science over the centuries? The authors of this paper explore the “demographic, institutional and economic characteristics of science’s top performers,” by analyzing a dataset of 982 such scientists across the centuries and find a general trend in the scientific elite towards more interdisciplinarity, institutional elitism and later in life. While it’s known that a lot of Nobel prizes come down to interdisciplinary degrees, 54% of the Nobel discoveries in the dataset were made by scientists who were awarded degrees in at least two different fields and that having an interdisciplinary background “provides scientists with the broad set of skills they need in order to come up with novel solutions to complex problems in any scientific field.” They also find that many of science’s achievements are concentrated in elite institutions, as “fully 30% of all our discoveries were produced by just the top 25 universities in the world. They also find that the “the common image of a young scientist making a major discovery for the first time at age 25 or 30 — is wrong. While such events are common in a single individual’s career, the age at which someone has their first major discovery is highly concentrated around ages in the 20s and has very long right tails with very few discoveries at ages 60 or more. Instead, there is a narrow “golden age” centered at around ages 35 to 45 during which scientists tend to have all their major discoveries.” The authors also find that women and non-Europeans are underrepresented and European scientists continue to be outperformed by North Americans, adding this to the list of issues with today’s global scientific system. They argue that the “increasing burden of knowledge” requires more time in school to learn new skills and also that scientists need “new kinds of institutions to support them” that counterbalance the dangers of “over-specialization” in modern scientific research. They therefore “argue that increases in funding for science provided by agencies that take into account the broad range of skills scientists possess and supports research in a wide range of fields would accelerate progress. So too would universities that open themselves up to new areas of research.”
